steps.title: Identify like terms
steps.content: In $9x^2+7x^2+9x$, the terms $9x^2$ and $7x^2$ are like terms (both have $x^2$). The term $9x$ has a different exponent and is unlike them.

steps.title: Combine like terms
steps.content: Add the coefficients of the $x^2$ terms and keep $9x$ unchanged:$$9x^2+7x^2+9x=(9+7)x^2+9x=16x^2+9x$$

steps.title: Eliminate the answer choices
steps.content:
• A) $63x^4+9x$: multiplies the coefficients and adds the exponents, but combining like terms keeps the exponent as $x^2$, not $x^4$.
  • B) $9x^2+16x$: adds the $x$ coefficient $9$ to the $x^2$ coefficient $7$, mixing unlike terms.
  • C) $25x^5$: treats $9x$ as $9x^2$ and adds all exponents, which is not how like terms combine.
  • D) $16x^2+9x$: correctly adds $9+7=16$ for the $x^2$ coefficient and leaves $9x$ alone.
That matches choice D — D is correct.
